structured_choices.data_source: Retrospective cohort of 500 melanoma patients from a single academic cancer center or multi-site consortium with consistent treatment protocols. Data spans 5 years of clinical records. Genomic data sourced from institutional sequencing (tumor DNA/RNA panels); clinical notes from pathology reports, treatment summaries, and follow-up records. All data collected prospectively at the point of clinical care.
structured_choices.label_definition: Treatment response classified into 4 ordered categories per RECIST 1.1 and immunotherapy-specific criteria: complete response (CR), partial response (PR), stable disease (SD), progressive disease (PD). Assigned by treating oncologist at 12 weeks post-treatment initiation, confirmed by radiologist review on imaging. Binarized for primary analysis into responder (CR+PR) vs. non-responder (SD+PD) for clinically meaningful comparison. Response status must be assigned before model training begins.
structured_choices.split_method: Stratified random split preserving the distribution of response class (responder/non-responder) and key demographic strata (age, stage, ECOG performance status) across train/validation/test. No temporal split used here because treatment response is a fixed outcome measured post-hoc; temporal ordering is not predictive information flow but rather documentation sequence. Justification: Stratification ensures each fold represents the population the model will encounter in deployment; random assignment within strata avoids selection bias. Split percentages: 60% train (n≈300), 20% validation (n≈100), 20% held-out test (n≈100). Random seed is fixed and documented.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing fit on training set only; transformations applied identically to validation and test. Genomic feature scaling (z-score normalization, batch correction if multi-site): fit on train genomic data. Clinical note preprocessing (tokenization, stop-word removal, vocabulary construction for NLP encoder): fit on train text corpus only. Missing genomic markers imputed using median from train set. Missing clinical notes handled by explicit masking tokens; missingness indicators included as features. No feature selection or dimensionality reduction fit on validation or test.
structured_choices.features: Genomic features: (1) Mutation burden (TMB), (2) key driver mutations (BRAF, NRAS, NF1, KIT status as binary indicators), (3) MSI/dMMR status, (4) HLA allotype predictions (when available), (5) tumor mutational signatures (fitted NMF or similar on training cohort). Clinical features: (1) ECOG performance status, (2) LDH level (normalized), (3) number of metastatic sites, (4) stage at diagnosis (TNM), (5) prior therapy exposure (yes/no). NLP features: Clinical notes encoded via a pretrained BioBERT or SciBERT fine-tuned on immunotherapy outcomes (or embeddings extracted from pretrained model without retraining on this cohort to avoid leakage). The clinical note encoder produces a fixed-length vector; these vectors are concatenated with genomic and tabular features. All features are generated before model training begins; no data-adaptive feature engineering during validation.
structured_choices.model: Multi-modal neural network: (1) Genomic branch: 2–3 fully connected layers (64–128 units, ReLU activation, batch norm, 0.3 dropout). (2) Clinical tabular branch: 2 fully connected layers (32–64 units, ReLU, batch norm, 0.3 dropout). (3) Clinical text branch: BioBERT encoder outputs passed through 1 FC layer (64 units, ReLU). (4) Fusion layer: concatenate outputs of all three branches, pass through 2 FC layers (128 units, ReLU, 0.3 dropout), output layer with sigmoid activation (binary responder/non-responder classification). Alternative: Gradient boosting baseline uses the same hand-engineered features (without text embeddings or a separate text encoder) to isolate the contribution of multi-modal fusion and learned representations. Hyperparameter tuning performed on validation set; final architecture and learning rates logged.
structured_choices.baseline: Genomic-only model: identical architecture to multi-modal model but excludes the clinical tabular branch and clinical text branch; only genomic features fed through the genomic branch, then directly to fusion/output layers. This baseline receives equivalent tuning effort (same hyperparameter search space, same compute budget, same validation protocol). Both models are initialized with the same seed and optimization (Adam, same learning rate schedule). Justification: This is the correct comparison because it isolates the contribution of clinical data (notes + tabular) while holding architecture complexity and optimization constant. A logistic regression on genomic features is not a fair baseline because it conflates feature representation with architectural capacity. An untuned baseline is not acceptable because it confounds tuning effort with model capability.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUROC). Justification: The oncology team's goal is to identify responders with high confidence to allocate resources and tailor therapy. AUROC captures discrimination ability across all classification thresholds and is robust to class imbalance (responders vs. non-responders), making it appropriate for threshold-sensitive deployment decisions. It does not require threshold specification upfront and allows clinicians to choose operating point based on risk tolerance post-hoc.
secondary_metrics: Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC) — more sensitive to minority class (responders) performance
Sensitivity at 90% specificity (to minimize false positives in a resource-constrained setting)
Calibration: Expected Calibration Error (ECE) — ensures predicted probabilities reflect true response likelihood for personalized treatment decisions
Cohen's kappa on both full 4-class and binarized response labels to assess inter-model agreement
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Validation set (n≈100) used to select final hyperparameters (learning rate, dropout, layer widths, early stopping patience). Grid search or Bayesian optimization over a pre-specified hyperparameter space. Primary metric on validation set drives model selection. The test set is not consulted during this process.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: Test set (n≈100) is held out completely until final model is frozen. A single evaluation run is performed on test data after all hyperparameter selection and model development concludes. Test set metrics are reported with 95% confidence intervals (bootstrapped, 1000 replicates, stratified by response class). No test-set tuning, no threshold optimization on test data, no selective reporting of test results.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Stratified split ensures balance of age, stage, and ECOG across folds, reducing bias from prognostic factors. Demographic variables (age, gender, stage, ECOG, prior therapy, LDH) are included as features, allowing the model to learn their associations with response while enabling post-hoc analysis of whether multi-modal gains hold after adjustment. Missing data mechanism assessed: if missingness is related to response (e.g., sicker patients have incomplete notes), explicit missingness indicators are included as features and reported. Subgroup analysis: performance stratified by stage, ECOG, TMB quartile, and BRAF status to detect if multi-modal gains are uniform or concentrated. This is a prediction study, not a causal inference study; the design does not establish whether clinical features causally influence response, only whether they improve prediction.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Multi-Modal Prediction of Immunotherapy Response in Melanoma

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

We will use a retrospective cohort of 500 melanoma patients from a single institution or multi-site consortium with standardized treatment protocols, spanning 5 years of clinical records. The data includes three modalities:

- **Genomic data**: Tumor mutational burden (TMB), driver mutations (BRAF, NRAS, NF1, KIT), MSI/dMMR status, HLA predictions, and mutational signatures derived from sequencing panels.
- **Clinical tabular data**: ECOG performance status, LDH level, number of metastatic sites, TNM stage, and prior therapy exposure.
- **Clinical notes**: Pathology reports and treatment summaries, processed via a pretrained BioBERT encoder to generate fixed-length feature vectors.

The outcome label is treatment response, classified per RECIST 1.1 at 12 weeks post-treatment initiation and confirmed by radiologist review. For primary analysis, response is binarized into responders (CR+PR) versus non-responders (SD+PD). All labels are assigned prospectively at the point of clinical care and frozen before model development begins.

### 2. Data Splitting Strategy

We employ a **stratified random split** to preserve the distribution of response class and key demographic strata (age, stage, ECOG) across train, validation, and test sets. Stratification is justified because it ensures each fold represents the patient population the model will encounter in deployment and avoids selection bias. We do not use a temporal split because treatment response is a fixed post-hoc outcome; temporal ordering reflects documentation sequence, not predictive information flow.

**Split allocation**: 60% training (n≈300), 20% validation (n≈100), 20% held-out test (n≈100). A fixed random seed is documented to ensure reproducibility.

### 3. Preprocessing and Feature Engineering

All preprocessing steps are **fit on the training set only** and applied identically to validation and test:

- **Genomic features**: Z-score normalization and batch correction (if multi-site data) fit on train genomic data.
- **Tabular clinical features**: Missing values imputed using the training set median; missingness indicators included as binary features.
- **Clinical notes**: Tokenized and encoded using a pretrained BioBERT model (not retrained on this cohort to avoid label leakage). The encoder produces a 768-dimensional vector per patient.

All features are generated before model training; no adaptive feature selection or dimensionality reduction is performed on validation or test data.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The **multi-modal model** is a neural network with three branches:

- Genomic branch: 2–3 fully connected layers (64–128 units, ReLU, batch norm, 0.3 dropout).
- Clinical tabular branch: 2 fully connected layers (32–64 units, ReLU, batch norm).
- Clinical text branch: BioBERT embeddings passed through 1 FC layer (64 units, ReLU).
- Fusion: Concatenated outputs through 2 FC layers (128 units, ReLU, 0.3 dropout), then sigmoid output for binary classification.

The **baseline model** is identical in architecture but uses genomic features only, excluding clinical tabular and text branches. This isolates the predictive contribution of clinical data while holding architectural capacity and tuning effort constant. Both models are optimized with Adam and the same learning rate schedule; hyperparameters are tuned on the validation set with an identical search space and compute budget.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric**: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUROC). AUROC measures discrimination ability across all classification thresholds and is robust to class imbalance, making it appropriate for the oncology team's goal of identifying responders to allocate resources and tailor therapy. It does not require upfront threshold specification and allows clinicians to choose an operating point based on risk tolerance.

**Secondary metrics**:
- Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC), sensitive to responder detection in the minority class.
- Sensitivity at 90% specificity, to minimize false positives in a resource-constrained setting.
- Expected Calibration Error (ECE), to ensure predicted probabilities reflect true treatment response likelihood.
- Cohen's kappa on binarized and 4-class response labels.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

The validation set (n≈100) is used to select final hyperparameters via grid search or Bayesian optimization. AUROC on the validation set drives model selection and early stopping. The test set is not consulted during development and is held out completely until final model freezing.

### 7. Test Evaluation and Reporting

Once the multi-modal and baseline models are finalized, a single evaluation pass is performed on the held-out test set. Test metrics are reported with 95% confidence intervals computed via stratified bootstrap (1000 replicates). **Subgroup analyses** stratify performance by stage, ECOG, TMB quartile, and BRAF status to assess whether multi-modal gains are uniform or concentrated in specific patient subgroups.

### 8. Confound Management

Stratification ensures demographic balance across folds. Demographic and prognostic variables (age, stage, ECOG, prior therapy, LDH) are included as model features, allowing the model to learn their associations with response. Missing data mechanisms are assessed; if missingness is informative (e.g., sicker patients have fewer notes), missingness indicators are included and reported. This design predicts treatment response; it does not establish causality.
